name: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.

profile.description: Cleveland-Cliffs Inc. is the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America, operating an integrated steelmaking platform from iron ore mining through finished steel products. The company serves automotive, infrastructure, manufacturing, and distribution end markets with hot-rolled, cold-rolled, coated, stainless, electrical, and plate steel products. Transformed from an iron ore mining company through acquisitions of AK Steel (2020) and ArcelorMittal USA (2020). Founded in 1847, headquartered in Cleveland, Ohio.

innovation.label: DEVELOPING
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Cleveland-Cliffs executed a dramatic strategic transformation under CEO Lourenco Goncalves, acquiring AK Steel and ArcelorMittal USA in 2020 to pivot from an iron ore mining company to the largest integrated flat-rolled steel producer in North America — a business model redefinition that opened entirely new revenue streams in automotive, infrastructure, and advanced steel products [1][2]. Recently announced expansion into rare earth minerals processing adds another strategic dimension. AI Deployment (0/2): No evidence of meaningful AI-powered products or quantified AI-driven operational savings in Cleveland-Cliffs' steelmaking or mining operations. Market Share (2/2): Dominant market position as the largest flat-rolled steel producer in North America with leading automotive steel market share, reinforced by multi-year fixed-price contracts signed with all major automotive OEMs for 2026, increasing market share and securing high-margin business [1]. Product Pipeline (1/2): Announced rare earth minerals processing expansion and new steel product development, but specific timelines and revenue visibility are limited beyond the existing automotive contract pipeline. R&D Intensity (0/1): R&D spending is at or below industry average for integrated steelmakers; the company's competitive advantage derives from scale and vertical integration rather than technology innovation.

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (2/4): Cleveland-Cliffs under Goncalves articulates a clear purpose of revitalizing American steelmaking, domestic manufacturing, and union steelworker employment — specific, differentiated from peers, and connected to a broader vision of industrial self-sufficiency [2]. Strategic decisions (opposing the Nippon Steel acquisition of US Steel, advocating for Section 232 tariffs) clearly trace to this purpose. However, the purpose is more nationalist-industrial than transcendent. Community Impact (1/2): Goncalves has cultivated a reputation as a 'particularly union-friendly executive,' maintaining productive labor relations with the United Steelworkers and investing in steelworker communities across the Midwest [2]. The company's sustainability report highlights community engagement and charitable partnerships, though specific dollar amounts are limited. Employee Retention (1/2): The 25,000-employee workforce benefits from union representation providing job security and competitive wages, and Goncalves's pro-union stance creates relative labor stability for the steel industry, though specific retention or tenure data is not disclosed. Insider Buying (1/2): Goncalves holds significant equity in the company and has maintained insider ownership, though no notable net insider buying was identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on financial metrics. Cleveland-Cliffs is in a cyclical trough with deeply negative profitability — ROE of -18.6% and profit margin of -6.4% reflect the challenging 2025 steel pricing environment, with FY2025 revenue declining 3% YoY to $18.6B and EPS of -$2.31 [1]. Both metrics score at floor (0.0). D/E of 129.3% is elevated but within the scoring range (score 0.74), reflecting $7.8B debt against just $45M cash — a concerning liquidity position [1]. Revenue growth of 6.3% YoY in Q1 2026 shows improvement driven by tariff-supported pricing, but the 3-year revenue CAGR of -6.8% reflects persistent revenue erosion from the 2022 peak of $23.0B. Forward P/E of 24.8x is above the 20x target but within range. The Forward PEG of 0.43 is extremely attractive, reflecting consensus expectations for significant earnings recovery as Section 232 tariffs at 50% support domestic steel pricing [1]. No dividend is currently paid — Cleveland-Cliffs suspended its dividend in 2020.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (8/9): Cleveland-Cliffs operates blast furnaces, iron ore mines, steel finishing lines, and a massive physical logistics network — AI fundamentally cannot substitute for the production of physical steel [1]. The steelmaking process requires extreme heat, raw materials handling, and precision metallurgy that are structurally immune to digital substitution. Additionally, Section 232 tariffs at 50% create a regulatory moat that AI cannot circumvent. The company's dominant automotive steel market share is protected by multi-year contracts and qualification requirements that take years to establish. AI could potentially optimize some steelmaking processes (predictive maintenance, yield optimization) but cannot replace the core business. AI Opportunity (0/1): No evidence of meaningful AI-generated revenue. Cleveland-Cliffs does not sell AI-related products or services, and no specific AI-driven operational savings have been quantified in public disclosures.

infinite_game.summary: Cleveland-Cliffs under Lourenco Goncalves presents a paradox — a genuine long-term vision for American steelmaking executed through aggressive, often combative tactics that reflect finite game thinking. Goncalves's 12-year transformation of the company from a struggling iron ore miner to the largest integrated steelmaker in North America demonstrates real strategic conviction and courage. However, his confrontational style with analysts, short-sellers, and competitors, combined with a leverage-heavy acquisition strategy that has left the balance sheet strained, reflects a leader who plays to win rather than to keep playing. Sinek would admire the cause but question the methods.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Goncalves has articulated a genuine and distinctive purpose — revitalizing American steelmaking, protecting union manufacturing jobs, and building domestic industrial self-sufficiency [2]. This is not a corporate platitude; it has driven every major strategic decision from the AK Steel and ArcelorMittal acquisitions to the advocacy for tariff protection and opposition to the Nippon Steel/US Steel deal. The purpose is specific, service-oriented (protecting American workers), and bigger than any single product. However, it has not been validated across leadership transitions or multi-decade consistency — Goncalves IS the cause, and it is unclear whether the company's purpose would survive his departure.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Goncalves executed two transformative acquisitions (AK Steel and ArcelorMittal USA) during the uncertainty of 2020, deploying billions in capital to fundamentally reshape the company [2]. His public advocacy for steel tariffs and opposition to the Nippon Steel acquisition of US Steel demonstrated willingness to take unpopular positions. However, these bold moves were funded with significant leverage ($7.8B debt, 129% D/E) that created balance sheet fragility — true courage invests in long-term capability without creating existential financial risk.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Goncalves's reputation as a 'particularly union-friendly executive' has created productive labor relations with the United Steelworkers, providing a foundation of trust with the 25,000-employee workforce [2]. The company has maintained stable labor agreements without major strikes or disruptions. However, Goncalves's confrontational public persona — 'castigating analysts and short-sellers' — suggests an aggressive leadership style that may not cultivate the psychological safety Sinek considers essential for high-trust teams.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): The transformation from a pure-play iron ore mining company to an integrated steelmaker represents a meaningful strategic pivot — Cleveland-Cliffs completely changed its business model, customer base, and industry positioning through the 2020 acquisitions [2]. The recent expansion into rare earth minerals processing demonstrates continued strategic evolution. However, these moves were acquisitive rather than organic, and the company remains fundamentally in the same industrial complex (metals/materials). Sinek would see adaptation, not transformation.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (0/1): Goncalves's competitive language is distinctly finite — he is known for aggressive public attacks on short-sellers, analysts who question his strategy, and competitors including his vocal opposition to the Nippon Steel acquisition of US Steel [2]. The company's competitive framing is about 'winning' market share and defeating foreign competitors rather than learning from their strengths. Sinek would identify this as a leader who competes to destroy rather than to improve.

investment_thesis: Cleveland-Cliffs is positioned for a significant earnings recovery driven by Section 232 tariffs at 50% on imported steel, which management expects to sustain domestic pricing and reduce import competition [1]. Q1 2026 revenue of $4.9B increased $600M sequentially, with CEO Goncalves declaring the 'order book is full' as steel prices rise. Multi-year fixed-price contracts with all major automotive OEMs secure high-margin business for 2026 and beyond. The Forward PEG of 0.43 is exceptionally attractive, reflecting consensus expectations for a sharp earnings inflection from the -$2.31 EPS in 2025 to positive territory in 2026. The company's position as the largest integrated flat-rolled steelmaker in North America, with vertically integrated operations from iron ore mining to finished products, creates structural cost advantages that tariff protection amplifies [1].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on cyclical vulnerability and balance sheet strain. Cleveland-Cliffs posted -$2.31 EPS in 2025 on $18.6B revenue, with negative ROE (-18.6%), negative profit margin (-6.4%), and negative free cash flow (-$60.5M) [1]. The balance sheet carries $7.8B debt against just $45M cash — a precariously thin liquidity cushion for a cyclical business with 129% D/E. The 3-year revenue CAGR of -6.8% from the 2022 peak of $23.0B reflects the steel cycle's brutality regardless of tariff support. Tariff-driven pricing improvements are inherently political and reversible — a change in administration or trade policy could rapidly compress steel premiums. No dividend is paid, providing no income floor for investors. The rare earth minerals expansion, while strategically interesting, is early-stage and capital-intensive at a time when the balance sheet can least afford it. Beta of 2.09 ensures extreme equity volatility in any market correction.
key_catalysts: Section 232 tariffs at 50% on imported steel driving domestic price recovery and margin expansion, with CEO Goncalves reporting a full order book and rising steel prices in Q1 2026
Multi-year fixed-price automotive OEM contracts securing high-margin revenue through 2026-2027, increasing market share in the most profitable end-market segment
Earnings inflection from -$2.31 EPS (2025) to consensus estimates of $0.50+ forward EPS as tariff-supported pricing flows through the P&L and volume recovers
key_risks: Extreme balance sheet vulnerability — $7.8B debt against $45M cash with 129% D/E creates existential risk in a prolonged steel downturn, limiting financial flexibility for capital allocation or acquisition opportunities

Tariff-dependent business model — Section 232 tariffs at 50% are a political instrument subject to reversal or reduction under future administrations, and any relaxation could rapidly compress domestic steel premiums

Cyclical earnings volatility with beta of 2.09 — the 3-year revenue decline of -6.8% CAGR and FY2025 losses of -$2.31 EPS demonstrate that even with structural advantages, Cleveland-Cliffs remains deeply exposed to steel pricing cycles
